reasoning: The EXPINF10YR rate dropped slightly in August 2025 to 2.278%, breaking a roughly 4-month streak of hovering around 2.30-2.35%. Because this metric measures long-term (10-year) expectations, it is deeply anchored by the Federal Reserve's long-term inflation target. Consequently, it exhibits both low volatility relative to observed inflation and meaningful mean reversion. At 2.278%, it is in the lower half of its range over the past 9 months and below its local average of 2.33%. This recent dip suggests a slight tendency for a bounce-back or mean reversion toward the ~2.30%+ level in the near term, generating a modest upward bias in the short term. The FRED statistical tool's trend analysis also assigns a 52% probability of exceeding the threshold. Over longer time horizons, increased uncertainty dictates that the probabilities should converge toward 0.50 as any immediate short-term mean reversion trend is washed out by macroeconomic unpredictability.
